scene_title: Troyes in Champagne. An apartment in the French King's palace

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Peace to this meeting, wherefore we are met!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Unto our brother France, and to our sister,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Health and fair time of day; joy and good wishes

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To our most fair and princely cousin Katharine;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And, as a branch and member of this royalty,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: By whom this great assembly is contrived,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: We do salute you, Duke of Burgundy;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And, princes French, and peers, health to you all!

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Right joyous are we to behold your face,

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Most worthy brother England; fairly met:

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: So are you, princes English, every one.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: So happy be the issue, brother England,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: Of this good day and of this gracious meeting,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: As we are now glad to behold your eyes;

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: Your eyes, which hitherto have borne in them

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: Against the French, that met them in their bent,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: The fatal balls of murdering basilisks:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: The venom of such looks, we fairly hope,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: Have lost their quality, and that this day

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: Shall change all griefs and quarrels into love.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To cry amen to that, thus we appear.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: You English princes all, I do salute you.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: My duty to you both, on equal love,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Great Kings of France and England! That I have labour’d,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: With all my wits, my pains and strong endeavours,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: To bring your most imperial majesties

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Unto this bar and royal interview,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Your mightiness on both parts best can witness.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Since then my office hath so far prevail’d

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: That, face to face and royal eye to eye,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: You have congreeted, let it not disgrace me,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: If I demand, before this royal view,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: What rub or what impediment there is,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Why that the naked, poor and mangled Peace,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Dear nurse of arts, plenties and joyful births,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Should not in this best garden of the world

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Our fertile France, put up her lovely visage?

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Alas, she hath from France too long been chased,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: And all her husbandry doth lie on heaps,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Corrupting in it own fertility.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Her vine, the merry cheerer of the heart,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Unpruned dies; her hedges even-pleach’d,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Like prisoners wildly overgrown with hair,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Put forth disorder’d twigs; her fallow leas

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: The darnel, hemlock and rank fumitory

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Doth root upon, while that the coulter rusts

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: That should deracinate such savagery;

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: The even mead, that erst brought sweetly forth

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: The freckled cowslip, burnet and green clover,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Wanting the scythe, all uncorrected, rank,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Conceives by idleness, and nothing teems

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: But hateful docks, rough thistles, kecksies, burs,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Losing both beauty and utility.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: And as our vineyards, fallows, meads and hedges,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Defective in their natures, grow to wildness,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Even so our houses and ourselves and children

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Have lost, or do not learn for want of time,

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: The sciences that should become our country;

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: But grow like savages—as soldiers will

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: That nothing do but meditate on blood—

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: To swearing and stern looks, diffused attire

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: And everything that seems unnatural.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Which to reduce into our former favour

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: You are assembled: and my speech entreats

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: That I may know the let, why gentle Peace

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Should not expel these inconveniences

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: And bless us with her former qualities.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If, Duke of Burgundy, you would the peace,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Whose want gives growth to the imperfections

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Which you have cited, you must buy that peace

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: With full accord to all our just demands;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Whose tenours and particular effects

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: You have enscheduled briefly in your hands.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: The king hath heard them; to the which as yet

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: There is no answer made.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Well, then, the peace,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Which you before so urged, lies in his answer.

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: I have but with a cursorary eye

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: O’erglanced the articles: pleaseth your grace

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: To appoint some of your council presently

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: To sit with us once more, with better heed

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: To resurvey them, we will suddenly

lines.speaker: FRENCH KING
lines.text: Pass our accept and peremptory answer.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Brother, we shall. Go, uncle Exeter,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And brother Clarence, and you, brother Gloucester,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Warwick and Huntington, go with the king;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And take with you free power to ratify,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Augment, or alter, as your wisdoms best

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Shall see advantageable for our dignity,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Anything in or out of our demands,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And we’ll consign thereto. Will you, fair sister,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Go with the princes, or stay here with us?

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: Our gracious brother, I will go with them:

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: Haply a woman’s voice may do some good,

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: When articles too nicely urg’d be stood on.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Yet leave our cousin Katharine here with us:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: She is our capital demand, comprised

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Within the fore-rank of our articles.

lines.speaker: QUEEN ISABEL
lines.text: She hath good leave.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Fair Katharine, and most fair,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Will you vouchsafe to teach a soldier terms

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Such as will enter at a lady’s ear

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And plead his love-suit to her gentle heart?

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Your majesty shall mock me; I cannot speak your England.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: O fair Katharine, if you will love me soundly with your French

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: heart, I will be glad to hear you confess it brokenly with

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: your English tongue. Do you like me, Kate?

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Pardonnez-moi, I cannot tell vat is “like me.”

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: An angel is like you, Kate, and you are like an angel.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Que dit-il? Que je suis semblable à les anges?

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Oui, vraiment, sauf votre grâce, ainsi dit-il.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I said so, dear Katharine; and I must not blush to affirm it.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: O bon Dieu! les langues des hommes sont pleines de tromperies.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: What says she, fair one? that the tongues of men are full of deceits?

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Oui, dat de tongues of de mans is be full of deceits:

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: dat is de princess.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The princess is the better Englishwoman. I’ faith, Kate, my wooing is

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: fit for thy understanding: I am glad thou canst speak no better

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: English; for, if thou couldst, thou wouldst find me such a plain

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: king that thou wouldst think I had sold my farm to buy

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: my crown. I know no ways to mince it in love, but

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: directly to say “I love you:” then if you urge me farther

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: than to say “do you in faith?” I wear out my suit.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Give me your answer; i’ faith, do: and so clap hands and

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: a bargain: how say you, lady?

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Sauf votre honneur, me understand vell.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Marry, if you would put me to verses or to dance for

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: your sake, Kate, why you undid me: for the one, I have

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: neither words nor measure, and for the other, I have no strength

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: in measure, yet a reasonable measure in strength. If I could win

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: a lady at leap-frog, or by vaulting into my saddle with my

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: armour on my back, under the correction of bragging be it spoken,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I should quickly leap into a wife. Or if I might buffet

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: for my love, or bound my horse for her favours, I could

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: lay on like a butcher and sit like a jack-an-apes, never off.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: But, before God, Kate, I cannot look greenly nor gasp out my

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: eloquence, nor I have no cunning in protestation; only downright oaths, which

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I never use till urged, nor never break for urging. If thou

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: canst love a fellow of this temper, Kate, whose face is not

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: worth sun-burning, that never looks in his glass for love of anything

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: he sees there, let thine eye be thy cook. I speak to

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: thee plain soldier: if thou canst love me for this, take me;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: if not, to say to thee that I shall die, is true;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: but for thy love, by the Lord, no; yet I love thee

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: too. And while thou livest, dear Kate, take a fellow of plain

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: and uncoined constancy; for he perforce must do thee right, because he

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: hath not the gift to woo in other places: for these fellows

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: of infinite tongue, that can rhyme themselves into ladies’ favours, they do

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: always reason themselves out again. What! a speaker is but a prater;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: a rhyme is but a ballad. A good leg will fall; a

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: straight back will stoop; a black beard will turn white; a curled

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: pate will grow bald; a fair face will wither; a full eye

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: will wax hollow: but a good heart, Kate, is the sun and

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: the moon; or rather the sun and not the moon; for it

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: shines bright and never changes, but keeps his course truly. If thou

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: would have such a one, take me; and take me, take a

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: soldier; take a soldier, take a king. And what sayest thou then

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: to my love? speak, my fair, and fairly, I pray thee.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Is it possible dat I sould love de enemy of France?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: No; it is not possible you should love the enemy of France,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Kate: but, in loving me, you should love the friend of France;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: for I love France so well that I will not part with

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: a village of it; I will have it all mine: and, Kate,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: when France is mine and I am yours, then yours is France

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: and you are mine.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: I cannot tell vat is dat.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: No, Kate? I will tell thee in French; which I am sure

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: will hang upon my tongue like a new-married wife about her husband’s

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: neck, hardly to be shook off. Je quand sur le possession de

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: France, et quand vous avez le possession de moi—let me see, what

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: then? Saint Denis be my speed!—donc votre est France et vous êtes

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: mienne. It is as easy for me, Kate, to conquer the kingdom

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: as to speak so much more French: I shall never move thee

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: in French, unless it be to laugh at me.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Sauf votre honneur, le françois que vous parlez, il est meilleur que

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: l’Anglois lequel je parle.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: No, faith, is’t not, Kate: but thy speaking of my tongue, and

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I thine, most truly-falsely, must needs be granted to be much at

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: one. But, Kate, dost thou understand thus much English, canst thou love

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: me?

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: I cannot tell.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Can any of your neighbours tell, Kate? I’ll ask them. Come, I

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: know thou lovest me: and at night, when you come into your

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: closet, you’ll question this gentlewoman about me; and I know, Kate, you

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: will to her dispraise those parts in me that you love with

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: your heart: but, good Kate, mock me mercifully; the rather, gentle princess,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: because I love thee cruelly. If ever thou beest mine, Kate, as

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I have a saving faith within me tells me thou shalt, I

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: get thee with scambling, and thou must therefore needs prove a good

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: soldier-breeder: shall not thou and I, between Saint Denis and Saint George,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: compound a boy, half French, half English, that shall go to Constantinople

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: and take the Turk by the beard? shall we not? what sayest

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: thou, my fair flower-de-luce?

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: I do not know dat.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: No; ’tis hereafter to know, but now to promise: do but now

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: promise, Kate, you will endeavour for your French part of such a

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: boy; and for my English moiety take the word of a king

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: and a bachelor. How answer you, la plus belle Katherine du monde,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: mon très cher et divin déesse?

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Your majestee ave fausse French enough to deceive de most sage demoiselle

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: dat is en France.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Now, fie upon my false French! By mine honour, in true English,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I love thee, Kate: by which honour I dare not swear thou

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: lovest me; yet my blood begins to flatter me that thou dost,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: notwithstanding the poor and untempering effect of my visage. Now, beshrew my

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: father’s ambition! he was thinking of civil wars when he got me:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: therefore was I created with a stubborn outside, with an aspect of

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: iron, that, when I come to woo ladies, I fright them. But,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: in faith, Kate, the elder I wax, the better I shall appear:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: my comfort is, that old age, that ill layer up of beauty,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: can do no more spoil upon my face: thou hast me, if

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: thou hast me, at the worst; and thou shalt wear me, if

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: thou wear me, better and better: and therefore tell me, most fair

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Katharine, will you have me? Put off your maiden blushes; avouch the

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: thoughts of your heart with the looks of an empress; take me

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: by the hand, and say “Harry of England, I am thine:” which

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: word thou shalt no sooner bless mine ear withal, but I will

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: tell thee aloud “England is thine, Ireland is thine, France is thine,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: and Henry Plantagenet is thine;” who, though I speak it before his

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: face, if he be not fellow with the best king, thou shalt

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: find the best king of good fellows. Come, your answer in broken

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: music; for thy voice is music and thy English broken; therefore, queen

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: of all, Katharine, break thy mind to me in broken English; wilt

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: thou have me?

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Dat is as it shall please le roi mon père.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Nay, it will please him well, Kate; it shall please him, Kate.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Den it sall also content me.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Upon that I kiss your hand, and call you my queen.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Laissez, mon seigneur, laissez, laissez: ma foi, je ne veux point que

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: vous abaissiez votre grandeur en baisant la main d’une votre seigneurie indigne

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: serviteur: excusez-moi, je vous supplie, mon très-puissant seigneur.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Then I will kiss your lips, Kate.

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: Les dames et demoiselles pour être baisées devant leurs noces, il n’est

lines.speaker: KATHERINE
lines.text: pas la coutume de France.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Madame my interpreter, what says she?

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Dat it is not be de fashion pour les ladies of France—I

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: cannot tell vat is baiser en Anglish.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: To kiss.

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Your majestee entend bettre que moi.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: It is not a fashion for the maids in France to kiss

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: before they are married, would she say?

lines.speaker: ALICE
lines.text: Oui, vraiment.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: O Kate, nice customs curtsy to great kings. Dear Kate, you and

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I cannot be confined within the weak list of a country’s fashion:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: we are the makers of manners, Kate; and the liberty that follows

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: our places stops the mouth of all find-faults; as I will do

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: yours, for upholding the nice fashion of your country in denying me

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: a kiss: therefore, patiently and yielding. Kissing her. You have witchcraft in

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: your lips, Kate: there is more eloquence in a sugar touch of

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: them than in the tongues of the French council; and they should

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: sooner persuade Harry of England than a general petition of monarchs. Here

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: comes your father.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: God save your majesty! my royal cousin, teach you our princess English?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I would have her learn, my fair cousin, how perfectly I love

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: her; and that is good English.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Is she not apt?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Our tongue is rough, coz, and my condition is not smooth; so

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: that, having neither the voice nor the heart of flattery about me,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I cannot so conjure up the spirit of love in her, that

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: he will appear in his true likeness.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: Pardon the frankness of my mirth, if I answer you for that.

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: If you would conjure in her, you must make a circle; if

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: conjure up love in her in his true likeness, he must appear

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: naked and blind. Can you blame her then, being a maid yet

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: ros’d over with the virgin crimson of modesty, if she deny the

lines.speaker: BURGUNDY
lines.text: appearance of a naked blind boy in her naked seeing self? It

lines.speaker: KING HENRY

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Then shall I swear to Kate, and you to me;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And may our oaths well kept and prosperous be! Sennet. Exeunt.

stage_directions.text: Enter, at one door, King Henry, Exeter, Bedford, Gloucester, Warwick, Westmoreland, and other Lords; at another, the French King, Queen Isabel, the Princess Katharine, Alice, and other Ladies; the Duke of Burgundy, and his train.
Exeunt all except Henry, Katharine, and Alice.
Reenter the French King and his Queen, Burgundy, and other Lords.

glosses.definition: for which we are gathered — Henry opens with formal diplomatic language
fellow king of France — 'brother' is the standard royal address between kings
Queen Isabel of Bavaria, Charles VI's wife
Princess Catherine of Valois — the betrothed
Philip III 'the Good' of Burgundy — historically not at Troyes; Shakespeare conflates him with his father John the Fearless
outcome; offspring — Queen Isabel's diplomatic pun
deadly cannonballs of killing serpents — a basilisk is both a mythical serpent whose look kills AND a large cannon
the poison of such glances — continuing the basilisk image

greeted each other (a Shakespearean coinage)

Peace nurses the arts, agriculture, and childbirth
France as Eden — the phrase the Epilogue will echo with bitter irony
rotting in its own abundance — neglected farmland goes to weed
hedges evenly braided (maintained by pleaching) — but now overgrown
fallow fields; unploughed meadows
weeds — rye-grass (darnel), poisonous hemlock, smoky-looking fumitory; Shakespeare's catalogue of agricultural neglect
the blade of a plough that cuts the furrow

annotations.title: Burgundy's lament for France — the garden-pastoral as political argument
annotations.body: The Duke of Burgundy's forty-five-line plea for peace is the scene's opening rhetorical set-piece, and one of the play's most celebrated passages. It is also a political argument disguised as pastoral description. Burgundy catalogues, at length, what war has done to the French countryside: the 'naked, poor and mangled Peace' exiled; vines unpruned and dying; hedges grown wild; meadows overrun with weeds ('darnel, hemlock and rank fumitory'); and — extending the image — houses, children, and citizens 'grow like savages' for want of the 'sciences that should become our country.' The passage is beautiful and persuasive. It is also politically precise: Burgundy, historically a key mediator between the English and French crowns, is arguing that peace at any price is necessary for French civic life. This is the kind of speech Henry needs to hear in order to move forward with the treaty. The scene's structural logic is that Burgundy's lyrical diplomacy softens the French, and Henry can then extract his terms (marriage, heir-status to the crown, Katherine as capital demand) against a background of visibly ravaged France. The garden-pastoral rhetoric is not neutral; it is a lever.
sources: Patricia Parker, Shakespeare from the Margins (1996)
Peter Saccio, Shakespeare's English Kings (2nd ed., 2000)

annotations.title: The wooing scene — Henry's 'plain soldier' pose as political performance
annotations.body: Henry's courtship of Katherine is the play's longest sustained scene and its most heavily choreographed prose passage. Henry casts himself repeatedly as a 'plain soldier' — unable to dance, unable to rhyme, unable to 'look greenly' or 'gasp out [his] eloquence' — and offers Katherine only 'downright oaths' and a 'good heart.' The rhetoric is, however, anything but plain. Henry delivers long, polished prose paragraphs; he demonstrates competent (if deliberately awkward) French; he cites Saint Denis and Saint George; he prophesies a 'boy, half French, half English' who will 'take the Turk by the beard.' The 'plain soldier' pose is itself a carefully composed rhetorical mode — one more register in a king who has been speaking in different registers to different audiences throughout the play. At the same time, the scene has genuine local charm. Katherine's 'Is it possible dat I sould love de enemy of France?' (line 155) cuts through the flirtation to the unresolved political heart of the marriage. Henry's answer is a conquering-king's joke ('I love France so well that I will not part with a village of it; I will have it all mine'). The mixing of the political and the erotic is the point: Katherine is explicitly 'our capital demand,' the head of the articles, and the wooing scene is a ceremony for terms already arranged. Readers should notice who does the talking (Henry almost entirely), how much of the scene is in Henry's voice, and how carefully the 'plain soldier' pose is kept up. The pose is convincing; it is also work.
sources: Karen Newman, Fashioning Femininity and English Renaissance Drama (1991)
Juliet Dusinberre, Shakespeare and the Nature of Women (1975)

annotations.title: 'Nice customs curtsy to great kings' — the wooing scene's operative phrase
annotations.body: When Katherine politely cites the French custom that ladies are not kissed before their wedding, Henry's response is one of the most quietly revealing lines in the play: 'O Kate, nice customs curtsy to great kings. Dear Kate, you and I cannot be confined within the weak list of a country's fashion: we are the makers of manners, Kate; and the liberty that follows our places stops the mouth of all find-faults.' The phrase 'makers of manners' is operative. Henry is declaring that royal prerogative overrides national custom — his and Katherine's, jointly. The line works as wooing (it assimilates Katherine to his royal freedom), as conquest (it overrides French custom with English-king prerogative), and as political theory (kings are not subject to cultural norms, they make them). The kiss that follows is both a romantic gesture and a small constitutional act. Critics sometimes hear the scene as charming; Shakespeare also gives us this line. Both hearings are correct; the scene is doing both kinds of work at once.
sources: Stephen Greenblatt, Will in the World (2004)

annotations.title: The Treaty of Troyes — what the wooing scene is serving
annotations.body: The scene's formal conclusion records the effective terms of the Treaty of Troyes (1420). Exeter reads out the one article the French king has balked at: Henry is to be named in all future French royal documents as 'Notre très-cher fils Henri, Roi d'Angleterre, Héritier de France' — 'Our most dear son Henry, King of England, Heir of France.' The French king is to disinherit his own son (the Dauphin of the play, historically the future Charles VII) and acknowledge Henry as heir to the French throne. This was the Treaty's actual most important clause. The wooing scene is thus ornamental around this political fact: Katherine is given to Henry as the dynastic seal of an agreement that makes Henry the next king of France. Historically, the Treaty held for barely two years: Henry V died of dysentery in August 1422, and his infant son Henry VI's regency promptly collapsed, enabling Charles VII's eventual reconquest (the era of Joan of Arc) and the loss of all French territory by 1453. The play does not mention any of this within the scene — it is reserved for the Epilogue. But the reader who holds the Treaty's actual history alongside Henry's closing 'Prepare we for our marriage' will hear the scene's final lines differently from a reader who does not.
sources: Anne Curry, The Hundred Years War (2nd ed., 2003)
Peter Saccio, Shakespeare's English Kings (2nd ed., 2000)

trials.prompt: What is Burgundy doing rhetorically in his long lament for France (lines 23–67)?
options: Genuinely grieving for French agriculture only

Delivering a political argument in pastoral disguise: the 'naked, poor and mangled Peace' and the overgrown vineyards are a plea that justifies French concessions on any terms. The beauty of the rhetoric is the lever
Confessing to treason
Proposing a Crusade

trials.prompt: How should we read Henry's 'plain soldier' pose in the wooing scene?
options: As literally plain, unrehearsed speech

As a carefully composed rhetorical register — Henry delivers long polished prose paragraphs while claiming plainness; the pose is one more audience-calibrated register in a king whose speech varies by audience throughout the play
As evidence he had no eloquence
As a sign of his youthful nerves

trials.prompt: What is the force of Henry's 'nice customs curtsy to great kings … we are the makers of manners' (lines 234–236)?
options: A tactful compliment to French tradition
An assertion of royal prerogative over cultural norm: kings aren't subject to custom, they create it. The line overrides Katherine's objection to pre-marriage kissing and doubles as political theory
A quotation from Chaucer
A piece of Welsh dialect

trials.prompt: What does Exeter read out at lines 285–288?
options: A prayer in French

The key clause of the Treaty of Troyes (1420): that Henry is to be named 'Héritier de France' — heir to the French throne — in all future French royal documents. The wooing scene ornaments this treaty-clause
Katherine's dowry inventory
A list of French prisoners still held in England

trials.prompt: What does the wooing scene's place in the play's argument invite us to see?
options: That love conquers all political conflict

That the marriage is a dynastic settlement dressed as romance: Katherine is the 'capital demand' of the treaty; the wooing scene is a ceremony for terms already arranged. Romance and conquest are not separable here
That Henry has given up his French claim
That Katherine was secretly Welsh

trials.prompt: Why does Katherine's 'Is it possible dat I sould love de enemy of France?' (line 155) matter?
options: It introduces Katherine as a minor character

It is the one moment the scene's underlying political question surfaces in Katherine's own voice. Henry's reply — loving him means loving France's conqueror, who will have 'all' of it — doesn't resolve the question so much as absorb it
It shows Katherine does not actually speak French
It is a quotation from Chaucer
